title: Iterative DFS (explicit stack)
code: if (node.right is not null) stack.Push(node.right); // right
if (node.left is not null) stack.Push(node.left);   // left

frames.caption: Push the root (5) onto an explicit stack, then loop until the stack is empty. The stack replaces recursion's call stack.
frames.note: The iterative form makes the implicit call stack explicit — the standard "now do it without recursion" whiteboard follow-up. We seed the stack with the root; from here a single while-loop drives the whole traversal, no recursion needed.

frames.caption: Pop the top node 5 → it is the current node. Stack is now [].
frames.note: A Stack<TreeNode> stands in for the recursion call stack: LIFO means the most recently pushed (deepest-pending) node comes back out first, which is exactly the deepest-first dive recursion would do. We pop that node and make it the current one — popping is "entering" the call, the same moment recursion would run the function body.

frames.caption: Visit 5: append it to the output immediately (preorder = Node first). Output: [5].
frames.note: Preorder is Node, Left, Right, so we record the node the instant we pop it — before touching either child. That is the whole difference from inorder/postorder: WHEN you emit relative to the recursive descents. The output grows in the same order the recursive PreorderIterative would produce.

frames.caption: 5 has a right child 8 → push it FIRST so it sits under the left and pops later. Stack: [8].
frames.note: Push the RIGHT child first. Because the stack is LIFO, whatever we push last comes out first; pushing right now means it sits UNDER the left child and waits. This is the one counter-intuitive line — to get left-before-right (preorder), you must stack them right-before-left.

frames.caption: 5 has a left child 3 → push it SECOND so it lands on top and pops NEXT. Stack: [8, 3].
frames.note: Push the LEFT child second so it lands on TOP of the stack and is the very next node popped. That makes the algorithm fully explore the left subtree before ever returning to the right one — mirroring how recursion calls Inorder(node.left) before node.right.

frames.caption: Pop the top node 3 → it is the current node. Stack is now [8].
frames.note: A Stack<TreeNode> stands in for the recursion call stack: LIFO means the most recently pushed (deepest-pending) node comes back out first, which is exactly the deepest-first dive recursion would do. We pop that node and make it the current one — popping is "entering" the call, the same moment recursion would run the function body.

frames.caption: Visit 3: append it to the output immediately (preorder = Node first). Output: [5, 3].
frames.note: Preorder is Node, Left, Right, so we record the node the instant we pop it — before touching either child. That is the whole difference from inorder/postorder: WHEN you emit relative to the recursive descents. The output grows in the same order the recursive PreorderIterative would produce.

frames.note: Push the RIGHT child first. Because the stack is LIFO, whatever we push last comes out first; pushing right now means it sits UNDER the left child and waits. This is the one counter-intuitive line — to get left-before-right (preorder), you must stack them right-before-left.

frames.caption: 3 has a left child 1 → push it SECOND so it lands on top and pops NEXT. Stack: [8, 4, 1].
frames.note: Push the LEFT child second so it lands on TOP of the stack and is the very next node popped. That makes the algorithm fully explore the left subtree before ever returning to the right one — mirroring how recursion calls Inorder(node.left) before node.right.

frames.caption: Pop the top node 1 → it is the current node. Stack is now [8, 4].

We pop that node and make it the current one — popping is "entering" the call, the same moment recursion would run the function body.

frames.caption: Pop the top node 9 → it is the current node. Stack is now [].
frames.note: A Stack<TreeNode> stands in for the recursion call stack: LIFO means the most recently pushed (deepest-pending) node comes back out first, which is exactly the deepest-first dive recursion would do. frames.caption: Visit 9: append it to the output immediately (preorder = Node first). Output: [5, 3, 1, 4, 8, 9].
frames.note: Preorder is Node, Left, Right, so we record the node the instant we pop it — before touching either child. That is the whole difference from inorder/postorder: WHEN you emit relative to the recursive descents. The output grows in the same order the recursive PreorderIterative would produce.

frames.caption: Stack empty → done. Preorder traversal emitted 5, 3, 1, 4, 8, 9 with no recursion.
frames.note: The stack is empty: every node was pushed once and popped once, so each is visited exactly once → O(n) time and O(h) stack space — the same asymptotics as recursion, but with no risk of a deep-recursion stack overflow on a skewed tree. The emitted order is the preorder 5, 3, 1, 4, 8, 9.
